title: Observation Shade
desc: The survey tent is useless if the shade roof sags and the sighting table rocks every time the ground starts shimmering.

steps.text:
The survey tent at Mipedim Mirage is not there to make anyone comfortable. It is there to create a small patch of stable judgment in a place that does not respect judgment much. Under the woven shade roof sit the line maps, weighted tablets, sighting rods, and the table where each morning's observations are compared against yesterday's before anyone decides where it is safe to walk. Today the roof sags on one corner and the table wobbles enough to make the recorder suspicious of every line she draws.

She presses a palm to the tabletop until the leg clicks against stone. "Patch the shade panels if you want to work overhead," she says. "Or level the sighting table and map weights if you prefer the instruments. Both matter. A bad roof distracts the eye. A bad table lies to the hand."

options.label: Patch the woven shade panels above the survey tent
Level the sighting table and reset the map weights

steps.text:
The shade roof has loosened where the binding cord dried and slipped during yesterday's heat. You climb the side frame, draw the woven panels tight again, and stitch a new brace through the frayed corner so the cloth pulls clean and even from post to post. Once the sag is gone, the light under the tent changes immediately. Harsh glare softens into a workable amber shade, and the maps stop flashing white whenever a breeze lifts the edge.

The recorder looks up from her tablets and relaxes by one visible degree. Out here, comfort is not luxury. It is accuracy made survivable. "Better," she says. "If the sun wants to distort everything, it can do so outside the tent."

options.label: Tie off the last brace and leave the roof holding firm

steps.text:
A rocking table in a mirage field is more dangerous than it sounds. Every notch copied wrong becomes a route argued over later. You shim the short leg with dressed stone wafers, square the surface, and reset the brass map weights so the cloth chart lies flat instead of creeping toward the low edge. Then you test the table yourself, leaning in at each corner until nothing shifts and the ink cup stays where it was told.

The recorder redraws one of the morning bearings on the newly settled surface and watches the line stay true all the way to the edge of the map. That is enough to restore her mood. "Good," she says. "If the horizon is going to move, the furniture should at least have principles."

options.label: Stack the spare shims and leave the station orderly
